data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Lear (LEA) After A 60% One Year Share Price Jump
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Lear's current share price lines up with its real worth, especially after a strong run, this article breaks down what the market might be pricing in and what that could mean for you.
  • Over the last year, Lear has returned 60.8%, with shorter-term moves of 3.7% over 7 days, 7.2% over 30 days, and 3.2% year to date from a last close of US$122.40, which can change how investors think about both opportunity and risk.
  • Recent coverage around Lear has focused on its position in the auto components space and how it is responding to ongoing sector trends, which helps explain why attention on the stock has stayed high. That context matters when you are judging whether the recent price action reflects fundamentals or just shifting sentiment.
  • Lear currently holds a value score of 5 out of 6, which suggests several traditional checks flag it as potentially undervalued and provides a starting point for a closer look at methods like discounted cash flow, market multiples, and a more holistic way of thinking about valuation later in this article.

Lear delivered 60.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Auto Components industry.

Approach 1: Lear Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a share could be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what future cash generated for shareholders is worth in today's dollars.

For Lear, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $508.7 million, and analyst and extrapolated estimates point to free cash flow of $618.5 million in 2026 and $727.7 million in 2030, all in US$. Simply Wall St uses analyst inputs where available and then extends the series to build a 10 year path of cash flows.

Discounting those projected cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of $206.91 per share. Compared to the recent share price of $122.40, the model suggests the stock is about 40.8% undervalued based on these assumptions and cash flow projections.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lear.

Approach 2: Lear Price vs Earnings

P/E is a common way to think about valuation for profitable companies because it links what you pay today to the earnings the company is already generating. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is assuming about future growth and risk; higher expected growth or lower perceived risk usually supports a higher “normal” P/E.

Lear currently trades on a P/E of 14.19x. That sits below the Auto Components industry average of 16.43x and well below the peer average of 22.74x, which at first glance can make the stock look inexpensive relative to both its sector and direct comparables.

Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” for each company. For Lear, this Fair P/E Ratio is 20.57x, based on factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margin, market cap and specific risks. This is designed to be more tailored than a simple comparison with industry or peers because it adjusts for the company’s own fundamentals rather than treating all businesses as identical. Comparing the Fair Ratio of 20.57x with the current P/E of 14.19x suggests the shares trade below what this framework would consider a fair level on earnings.

Result: UNDERVALUED

You can then link that story to a forecast and finally to a fair value that can be compared with today’s share price on Simply Wall St’s Community page, where Narratives are available to millions of investors, update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive, and can range from a more cautious Lear view that lines up with a Fair Value of about US$123 up to a more optimistic view closer to US$174. data.title: Be Sure To Check Out Costamare Inc. (NYSE:CMRE) Before It Goes Ex-Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Costamare Inc. (NYSE:CMRE) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date occurs one day before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. In other words, investors can purchase Costamare's shares before the 20th of April in order to be eligible for the dividend, which will be paid on the 5th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.115 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$0.46 to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Costamare has a trailing yield of approximately 2.6% on its current stock price of US$17.40. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Costamare is paying out just 15% of its profit after tax, which is comfortably low and leaves plenty of breathing room in the case of adverse events. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Costamare generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. It distributed 33% of its free cash flow as dividends, a comfortable payout level for most companies.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

View our latest analysis for Costamare

Click here to see how much of its profit Costamare paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. With that in mind, we're encouraged by the steady growth at Costamare, with earnings per share up 9.2% on average over the last five years. The company is retaining more than half of its earnings within the business, and it has been growing earnings at a decent rate. Organisations that reinvest heavily in themselves typically get stronger over time, which can bring attractive benefits such as stronger earnings and dividends.

We'd also point out that Costamare issued a meaningful number of new shares in the past year. Trying to grow the dividend while issuing large amounts of new shares reminds us of the ancient Greek tale of Sisyphus - perpetually pushing a boulder uphill.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Costamare's dividend payments per share have declined at 8.8% per year on average over the past 10 years, which is uninspiring. Costamare is a rare case where dividends have been decreasing at the same time as earnings per share have been improving. It's unusual to see, and could point to unstable conditions in the core business, or more rarely an intensified focus on reinvesting profits.

The Bottom Line

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Costamare? Earnings per share growth has been growing somewhat, and Costamare is paying out less than half its earnings and cash flow as dividends. This is interesting for a few reasons, as it suggests management may be reinvesting heavily in the business, but it also provides room to increase the dividend in time. We would prefer to see earnings growing faster, but the best dividend stocks over the long term typically combine significant earnings per share growth with a low payout ratio, and Costamare is halfway there. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.

In light of that, while Costamare has an appealing dividend, it's worth knowing the risks involved with this stock. For instance, we've identified 3 warning signs for Costamare (1 is concerning) you should be aware of.

data.title: Tesla’s EU FSD Breakthrough Puts Software Valuation Story in Focus
data.description:

  • This is the first step that could allow Tesla's supervised autonomous driving system to be legally offered across the European Union.

Tesla (NasdaqGS:TSLA) is drawing fresh attention as regulators in the Netherlands back its Full Self-Driving supervised system, potentially opening a new chapter for its software and autonomy ambitions. The company’s share price is $364.2, with a 7 day return of 6.1% and a 1 year return of 50.8%, while the year to date return is a 16.9% decline. Over 3 years, the stock shows a 101.7% return and over 5 years a 46.8% return, highlighting how volatile the ride has been for shareholders.

If EU member states ultimately approve wider use of Full Self-Driving supervised technology, investors may start paying closer attention to how much of Tesla’s value is attributed to software and services rather than vehicle sales alone. For now, this Dutch decision mainly signals that regulators are willing to test and evaluate Tesla’s autonomy offering at a broader scale, which could influence how you think about the company’s potential revenue mix and risk profile.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Commvault Systems (CVLT) After Recent Share Price Rebound?

Approach 1: Commvault Systems Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth in current dollars.

For Commvault Systems, the model uses Free Cash Flow to Equity, starting from last twelve month free cash flow of about $183.1 million. Analysts provide explicit projections for several years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further cash flows out to 2035 using a 2 stage framework. Within those projections, free cash flow is modeled to reach $511.0 million in 2031, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2027 at $214.9 million and $264.1 million respectively, all in $ and then discounted back to today.

Adding up these discounted cash flows gives an estimated intrinsic value of $185.16 per share. Against a current share price around $89.61, the DCF output indicates a 51.6% discount on this model.

In broad terms, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually go with a lower P/E. So the question is not whether a P/E is high or low in isolation, but whether it fits the company’s profile.

That combination helps explain why some investors have treated the stock cautiously, even though the company controls 10,000 historical racing machines across 16 locations and operates 10 gaming venues in nine states. data.description:
Lacto Japan (TSE:3139) has opened fiscal 2026 with Q1 revenue of ¥48.6b and basic EPS of ¥77.17, setting the stage against a backdrop of steady earnings expansion over recent years. The company has seen revenue move from ¥45.0b in Q1 2025 to ¥48.6b in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from ¥433.16 to ¥389.62, as earnings growth of 10.1% over the last year meets more moderate forward forecasts. That mix of higher sales, evolving earnings trends and modest net margins gives investors a clear read on how efficiently Lacto Japan is turning top line into bottom line today.

With the latest results on the table, the next step is to set these numbers against the dominant market narratives around Lacto Japan to see which views hold up and which ones need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM earnings and margins steady at around 2%

  • Over the last 12 months, Lacto Japan generated ¥185,404 million in revenue and ¥3,885 million in net income, which works out to a 2.1% net margin compared with 2.0% in the prior year.
  • Supporters of a more optimistic view point to 10.1% earnings growth over the past year and a 17.7% annualised earnings growth rate over five years. However, the relatively slim 2.1% margin and revenue growth forecast of about 4.6% a year keep expectations grounded.
    • That mix of double digit earnings growth and a low single digit revenue outlook means the bullish angle leans heavily on efficiency and earnings quality rather than rapid top line expansion.
    • The small move in margin from 2.0% to 2.1% shows progress is measurable but still leaves the business operating on thin profitability, which bullish investors need to keep front of mind.

P/E of 9.1x versus higher market multiples

  • Lacto Japan trades at a P/E of 9.1x, which sits below the JP Consumer Retailing industry average of 13.1x, the peer average of 11.6x, and the broader JP market at 14.7x, while trailing twelve month EPS stands at ¥389.62.
  • Fans of the bullish angle often highlight this valuation gap as a sign of potential value. Yet the forecast earnings growth of about 4.4% a year and modest 2.1% net margin show that the lower multiple lines up with a steady rather than high growth profile.
    • The current share price of ¥3,550 alongside EPS of ¥389.62 is consistent with that 9.1x P/E, which is meaningfully below the wider market level of 14.7x.
    • At the same time, revenue of ¥185,404 million on a trailing basis is paired with only ¥3,885 million of net income, so the valuation story sits alongside a business that converts a relatively small slice of sales into profit.

Cash flow coverage flags key financial risks

  • On top of the 3.72% dividend yield, the company is flagged for two important coverage issues: debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and the dividend is not well covered by free cash flow, even though earnings grew 10.1% over the year.
  • Skeptical investors focus on these cash related risks, arguing that thin 2.1% net margins and only modest forecast earnings growth of about 4.4% a year limit flexibility if operating cash flow stays weak relative to debt and dividends.
    • The combination of a 3.72% yield and net income of ¥3,885 million on ¥185,404 million of revenue underlines that the company is paying out cash on a fairly narrow profit base.
    • While past earnings growth has averaged 17.7% a year over five years, the flagged shortfall in operating and free cash flow coverage shows that historical profit expansion does not automatically translate into stronger cash support for the balance sheet or payouts.

data.title: Unisia Holdings Q1 Profit Return Challenges Bearish Volatility Narratives
data.description:
Unisia Holdings (TSE:3547) opened Q1 2026 with revenue of ¥8.6b and basic EPS of ¥2.96, against a backdrop of trailing 12 month EPS of ¥68.18 and revenue of ¥25.1b that reflects a very large year on year earnings jump of about 100.2%. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from ¥4.6b in Q1 2025 to ¥5.7b in Q2, ¥5.3b in Q3 and ¥5.5b in Q4, while basic EPS ranged from ¥13.26 to ¥53.12 before swinging to a loss of ¥11.05 in Q4. This latest print therefore lands in the context of already volatile earnings. With trailing net margin at 2.6% versus 1.9% a year earlier, these results point to gradually firmer profitability even if the margin profile still leaves plenty of room for improvement.

See our full analysis for Unisia Holdings.

The next step is to set these fresh earnings against the most common stories around Unisia Holdings and see which narratives the numbers support and which they start to challenge.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM earnings up 100.2% but margin only 2.6%

  • Over the last twelve months, net income reached ¥654.1m on revenue of ¥25.1b, which translates into a 2.6% net margin compared with 1.9% a year earlier and earnings growth of about 100.2% year on year.
  • What stands out for a bullish view that focuses on earnings strength is that very large profit growth sits on quite a thin margin, which leaves room for debate:
    • Supporters can point to the move from a loss of ¥101.5m in Q4 2025 to profit of ¥32m in Q1 2026 and to the five year annualized earnings growth of 44.6% as signs that the business model can produce profits across different conditions.
    • On the other hand, critics can highlight that a 2.6% net margin on ¥25.1b of revenue still means most of the top line is absorbed by costs, so any bump in expenses could quickly eat into that ¥654.1m profit.

Volatile EPS path despite 68.18 TTM figure

  • Basic EPS over the last six reported quarters moved from a loss of ¥4.85 in Q4 2024 to ¥13.26, ¥53.12, ¥25.59 and a loss of ¥11.05 through 2025, before landing at ¥2.96 in Q1 2026, even as trailing twelve month EPS stands at ¥68.18.
  • Bears who focus on earnings stability get some backing from this pattern, yet also run into a few challenges from the same data:
    • The sequence that includes two loss making quarters and large swings between ¥53.12 and a loss of ¥11.05 supports the cautious view that profitability can move around sharply from one period to the next.
    • At the same time, the fact that these ups and downs still add up to ¥68.18 of trailing EPS and ¥654.1m of trailing net income means the longer look is less weak than the most recent quarterly dip alone might suggest.

Mixed valuation, with 32.7x P/E above DCF value

  • The shares trade at ¥1,977 with a P/E of 32.7x, which is below the 65x peer average but above the 22.8x JP Hospitality average, and compared with a DCF fair value of ¥120.55 and an analyst price target of ¥2,300.00.
  • For anyone weighing a bullish argument against a more cautious one, these numbers draw a clear tension between growth and pricing:
    • Supporters can highlight the forecast revenue growth of about 22% per year and forecast earnings growth of about 11.8% per year, along with the 100.2% trailing earnings uplift, as reasons the market may be comfortable assigning a higher P/E than the broader industry.
    • More cautious investors can point out that the P/E is still below peers even though the share price sits well above the DCF fair value of ¥120.55, while debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and a one off loss of ¥257.4m affected the last twelve months, all of which keeps the risk side of the equation firmly in view.
